questions.question: Explain the difference between single-bit error and burst error in data transmission. Give examples of each.
questions.hint: Think about noise types - white noise causes single-bit, impulse noise causes burst errors
keyPoints: Single-bit error: only one bit changes in data unit
Burst error: multiple bits affected, contiguous sequence
Single-bit caused by white noise
Burst caused by impulse noise, fading
Burst length measured from first to last error bit

questions.question: What is vertical redundancy check (VRC) in error detection? Explain its working with an example.
questions.hint: Consider a 7-bit ASCII character with an added parity bit
keyPoints: VRC adds parity bit to each character/data word
Even or odd parity can be used
Even parity: total 1s including parity is even
Odd parity: total 1s including parity is odd
Detects odd number of bit errors
Cannot detect even number of errors

questions.question: Describe the concept of checksum in error detection. How is it different from parity checking?
questions.hint: Think about how segment sums and complements provide better coverage
keyPoints: Checksum uses complement arithmetic
Data divided into segments, summed, complement sent
Detects multiple bit errors unlike single parity
More robust than VRC/LRC
Used in IP, TCP, UDP protocols
Can detect more error patterns

questions.question: Explain the Stop-and-Wait ARQ protocol for error control. Discuss its advantages, disadvantages, and the concept of sequence numbers and acknowledgments.
questions.hint: Draw a timing diagram showing frame transmission and acknowledgment
keyPoints: Sender waits for ACK before sending next frame
Uses sequence numbers (0 and 1) to identify frames
Timeout mechanism for lost frames
ACK can be positive or negative (NAK)
Simple to implement but inefficient
Channel utilization is low due to waiting
Suitable for reliable noisy channels

questions.question: Compare Go-Back-N ARQ and Selective Repeat ARQ protocols. Under what conditions is each preferred?
questions.hint: Consider the trade-off between complexity and channel efficiency
keyPoints: Go-Back-N: retransmits all frames from error point
Selective Repeat: retransmits only erroneous frame
Go-Back-N uses smaller sequence number space (N)
Selective Repeat needs larger sequence space
Go-Back-N simpler, less buffering
Selective Repeat more efficient for high error rates
Go-Back-N suitable for low error channels

questions.question: In a Stop-and-Wait ARQ system, the frame transmission time is 10 ms and the acknowledgment propagation delay is 5 ms. Calculate the utilization efficiency if the timeout value is set to 50 ms.
questions.hint: Utilization = Frame transmission time / Total cycle time
keyPoints: Calculate round trip time (RTT): 10 + 5 + 10 + 5 = 30 ms
Or simplified: transmission + propagation + ACK transmission + propagation
Utilization U = Transmission time / (Transmission + Timeout)
Or using RTT: U = Tt / (Tt + 2Tp + Ta)
Explain why efficiency is low in stop-and-wait

questions.question: What is the purpose of the frame sequence number in sliding window protocols? How many bits are needed for a Go-Back-N protocol with window size 8?
questions.hint: Window size N requires modulo N sequence numbers
keyPoints: Sequence numbers identify each frame uniquely
Enable receiver to detect duplicate frames
Support ordered delivery of frames
For Go-Back-N: sequence numbers range 0 to N-1
Need ceil(log2 N) bits
For N=8, need 3 bits (2^3 = 8)

questions.question: Explain the working of CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check) as an error detection method. How does it detect burst errors?
questions.hint: CRC-32, CRC-16, CRC-8 are common polynomials used
keyPoints: CRC uses polynomial division
Generator polynomial of degree n provides n+1 bits
Sender appends n remainder bits to data
Receiver divides received code by same generator
Non-zero remainder indicates error
Detects all burst errors of length <= degree of polynomial
Detects single-bit, double-bit, odd number of errors

questions.question: Differentiate between error detection and error correction. Which methods provide error correction and why?
questions.hint: Think about the redundancy needed to locate and fix errors
keyPoints: Error detection only identifies presence of errors
Error correction identifies location and corrects
Parity, CRC, Checksum provide detection only
Hamming code provides single-bit error correction
Convolution codes and Reed-Solomon provide correction
Error correction requires more redundancy

questions.question: Calculate the Hamming code for the data word 1101 using even parity. Show the parity bit positions and the final encoded word.
questions.hint: Hamming code positions: 1,2,3,4,5,6,7 where 1,2,4 are parity
keyPoints: Data bits: D3=1, D2=1, D1=0, D1=1 (positions 3,5,6,7)
Parity bits at positions 1,2,4 (P1,P2,P4)
P1 covers positions 1,3,5,7: 1,0,1 = even(2) so P1=0
P2 covers positions 2,3,6,7: 1,0,1 = even(2) so P2=0
P4 covers positions 4,5,6,7: 0,1,1 = even(2) so P4=0
Codeword: 1100101 or P1P2D3P4D2D1D3

questions.question: Discuss the various types of errors that can occur in data communication and the corresponding error control mechanisms used in the Data Link Layer.
questions.hint: Consider the three main problems: lost, damaged, and duplicate data
keyPoints: Errors: lost frames, damaged frames, duplicate frames
Lost frames: timeout and retransmission
Damaged frames: error detection (CRC) and discard
Duplicate frames: sequence numbers prevent
Flow control prevents overwhelming receiver
ARQ combines error detection and retransmission
Positive ACK confirms receipt, NAK requests retransmission

questions.question: In a sliding window protocol with window size 7, if the sender has sent frames 0 through 5 and receives an ACK for frame 3, what is the current window? How many more frames can be sent before requiring acknowledgment?
questions.hint: Sliding window moves forward as ACKs are received
keyPoints: Window size N = 7
Sender has sent frames 0,1,2,3,4,5 (6 frames)
ACK for frame 3 means frame 3 and before are acknowledged
Window slides, lower edge now at frame 4
Available sequence numbers: 4,5,6,0,1,2 (7 slots)
One more frame (frame 6) can be sent before ACK needed

questions.question: What is the difference between positive acknowledgment and negative acknowledgment in error control? When is each used?
questions.hint: Think about how each type of acknowledgment affects protocol efficiency
keyPoints: Positive ACK (ACK): frame received correctly
Negative ACK (NAK): frame error detected, request retransmission
ACK can be cumulative (acks multiple frames)
NAK provides faster error recovery
Some protocols use only ACK with timeout
NAK reduces waiting time for error recovery

questions.question: Design an error control strategy for a high-speed data link operating in a noisy environment. Justify your choice of protocol parameters.
questions.hint: Consider both error rate and propagation delay in protocol design
keyPoints: For noisy channel: Selective Repeat preferred over Go-Back-N
Use larger window size for better throughput
Implement flow control with sliding window
Use strong CRC for error detection (CRC-32)
Consider hybrid ARQ for efficiency
Balance between retransmission overhead and throughput
Account for propagation delay in timeout calculation

questions.question: Explain the concept of pipelining in error control protocols. How does it improve channel utilization compared to Stop-and-Wait?
questions.hint: Compare the timeline of frame transmissions in both approaches
keyPoints: Pipelining: multiple frames in transit simultaneously
Stop-and-wait: one frame at a time, low utilization
Pipelining overlaps transmission and propagation
Requires larger sequence number space
Go-Back-N and Selective Repeat are pipelined
Efficiency improves significantly for long delay paths
Trade-off: more complex error recovery
